entries: Bloomburrow is a charming pastoral plane full of anthropomorphic animals, featured in MAGIC: THE GATHERING. No human has set foot on the plane; anyone who travels to Bloomburrow is transformed into one of the animalfolk. The animalfolk have advanced culture, and unlike animalfolk from other planes, they retain a size normal for their species.

entries.name: About this Book
entries: This book is an in-depth guide to using MAGIC: THE GATHERING's plane of Bloomburrow as a setting for a Dungeons & Dragons 5 edition campaign. It guides players and the Dungeon Master through the process of creating characters and adventures set in this world. It is divided into the following sections.
{@book Chapter 1|blbcs|1} provides an overview to life in Bloomburrow.
{@book Chapter 2|blbcs|2} describes several locations in Valley, the home of Bloomburrow's animalfolk.
{@book Chapter 3|blbcs|3} details how to create Bloomburrow characters.
{@book Chapter 4|blbcs|4} presents the sixteen new species of Bloomburrow.
{@book Chapter 5|blbcs|5} provides information for character Classes on Bloomburrow, and presents four new or updated subclass options.
{@book Chapter 6|blbcs|6} presents eighteen background options for characters native to Bloomburrow.
{@book Chapter 7|blbcs|7} describes seven feats that characters can choose from.
{@book Chapter 8|blbcs|8} describes equipment unique to Bloomburrow, including tools, adventuring gear, and magic items.
{@book Chapter 9|blbcs|9} describes how magic is changed on Bloomburrow, and presents fifteen new spells.
{@book Appendices A and B|blbcs|10} present 22 creature stat blocks useful for players, and seven Monster stat blocks for DMs.

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: Bloomburrow is inhabited by a number of sapient animalfolk species, who live alongside other creatures such as insects and fish. They also share their home of Valley with elemental monsters known as Calamity Beasts. The inhabitants experience a relative peace as they learn to alongside other animalfolk and the forces of nature.

entries.name: History
entries: In Bloomburrow's distant past, the Calamity Beasts were common sights, wreaking havoc in a time known as the Season of Chaos. In this era, the animalfolk survived buried underground until they banded together to form the Order of the Holly Leaf.

entries.name: War of Cinders
entries: From this point animalfolk began to develop magic — later known as weaving — to protect their companions, and had their first victories against the weaker Calamity Beasts. Battles raged for two generations, until Wildsear, the wildfire wolf, turned the tide against the animalfolk. Lily of Valley, the champion of the Order of the Holly Leaf, wielded the Seed Flame to weaken the Calamity Beasts. In the final battle of the war, she drove the weakened Wildsear away from Valley and fashioned the sword Cragflame from its broken tooth.

entries.name: Weavers' Calamity
entries: Weavers grew in power, protecting Valley from the weakened Calamity Beasts. ver time, magic drove some weavers mad, turning them into monsters fueled by rage. They rampaged unstoppably, threatening the newly-achieved peace. This led to the great weavings being sealed, in favor of small spells anyone could manage.

entries.name: Foundation of Valley
entries: Once the powerful weaving was gone, the Calamity beasts returned to Valley. However, the animalfolk learned to deal with them by using small weaving in innovative ways. This specialization led to the formation of the actual civilization of Valley as each species of animalfolk learned to work together.

entries.name: Currency
entries: Barter is the preferred method of trade on Bloomburrow, with many merchants and cultures valuing raw goods like food and plant materials. Bloomburrow does have other treasures, including plain gold coins and gemstones which can be used to purchase goods.

entries.name: Languages
entries: Nearly all the animalfolk in Valley speak a common language. Each species also has its own ancestral language, though these languages are only used in ancient writings.

entries.name: Transportation
entries: The Otterfolk build playful houseboats that are linked in groups called Rafts that they ride up and down the rivers — an otterfolk can easily go its whole life without ever setting foot on solid ground.

entries.name: Astronomy & Religion
entries: The Cosmos contains spiritual wisdom passed on by the batfolk of past generations who have joined their ancestors in the night sky. Because of this, the stars of Bloomburrow are primarily tracked and observed by batfolk astronomers. Each star is given a name befitting its grandeur. This reverence for the Cosmos forms the basis of the Batfolk religion; the only one of its kind on Bloomburrow. Churches in cities often double as observatories.

Bloomburrow has one moon. At the end of each of the four moon phases (new, waxing, full, waning), batfolk across Valley host a celebration known as a Lunar Paean. Batfolk use the Lunar Paean to share their beliefs and communicate a respect for the cycle of life and death, light and dark. The batfolk are frequently joined by other denizens of Valley who were born during the same lunar pattern as when the ritual is held. Fortunes are performed for those present, and children born during the current phase are assigned star charts, while their parents are given guidance to help raise them. The celebration concludes with a moon-watching ceremony where families can look out into the firmament and assign stars to recently deceased loved ones or find the stars of their ancestors.

entries.name: Entertainment
entries: Quilting is a common communal activity, and communities and adventuring parties have a shared quilt composed of squares contributed by each family or party member, respectively. Frogfolk play a game called bubbleball. Otterfolk play an enigmatic game known as otterball, which the other animalfolk have little understanding of.

entries.name: Seasons & Calamity
entries: Calamity Beasts are huge, non-sapient elemental creatures that bring seasonal changes and weather variations. They wreak disaster on an unprecedented scale while they move through the landscape. They are essentially living seasons, and every bit as much part of nature as the animalfolk.

While some seasons are known to occur in Valley annually, others occur only once in a generation. The seasons are brought upon by the movement of a Calamity Beast, as shown in the Valley Seasons table.

data.name: Land of Valley
entries: Valley is the rural and vibrant home of the plane's animalfolk. A small region in the larger plane of Bloomburrow, Valley is a temperate stretch of land, home to a diverse array of wildlife and natural landmarks. Within Valley are five biomes: meadows, ponds, marshes, hills, and thickets. The lands outside Valley's villages and towns are known as the Brambles.

Meadows are characterized by flowers and grasses dotted with animalfolk farms. Ponds are freshwater, fed by rainfall, and are popular locations for ﬁshing. Marshes teem with insect life, especially in the tunnels and subterranean caverns below. The hills have sparse flora, and their ample sunlight warms Valley's lizardfolk. Forests are lush, holding enormous, ancient trees that house cities of animalfolk.

entries.name: Three Tree City
entries: Three Tree City, or Three Trees City, is the largest settlement in Valley; a forest city formed by the intertwining of an ancient oak, sycamore, and willow tree on the banks of the Long River, which runs the length of Valley from its source above the forests and empties into the vast marshlands. Buildings and fungal paths decorate its canopy and trunk. The city was founded to harmonize with nature, which is apparent in its organic layout. The city is divided into several districts.

entries.name: The Docklands
entries: The Docklands is a sprawling array of docks and rafts off the bank of the Long River. It is the center of trade for Three Tree City. This district lies under the tendrils of the Willow.

entries.name: The Quilted District
entries: The Quilted District lies in the city's heart. It is named after its vast quilted banner symbolizing the union of all animalfolk, featuring squares from every species that visits the city.

entries.name: Fountainport
entries: Fountainport is a city formed as a three-tiered, soapstone-carved fountain that rises from Valley's largest pond. At its pinnacle lies a sculpted flower and a magically suspended spire of water. Portcullises gate the city, and glass elevators, slides, and waterfalls connect the different levels. The second tier of the city holds domed frogfolk houses that sit upon individual bowls and more elegant boats than the lower levels. On the top level, the weaving of water magic supplements mundane architecture.
The Tadpool Harbor District is a rowdy district at the fountain's base. It consists of markets on floating docks and lily pads.

entries.name: Goodhill
entries: Goodhill is a town with white-petal roofs and woven grass walls interspersed with larger, painted clay burrows and a tall windmill. Colorful glass rainwater collectors line its cobbled street. Towering above it all, the wooden homes of batfolk stand atop long poles. Goodhill has a diverse population of many species. It is a two-day ride from Fountainport.

entries.name: The Knowledge Ossuary
entries: The Knowledge Ossuary is a limestone cavern village inhabited by ratfolk archivists. The most ancient Ossuary in Valley, it houses a library of the plane's history. It is protected by powerful enchantments and warriors, and only a ratfolk elder can permit a visitor to view the contents of the Ossuary. One of its entrances along the Long River is covered by a mussel shell, and its tunnels are illuminated by enchanted lights. Long ago, it was burrowed by an extinct species of enormous insects. It is west-southwest of Fountainport.

entries.name: The Hanging Gardens
entries: The Hanging Gardens rest in a shallow cenote behind a waterfall whose walls are covered with foliage that drapes down to touch the water's surface. Lily pads ﬂoat freely on the surface of the pool, and frogfolk come to bask in the sun at midday when the sun is right above its opening.

entries.name: Calamity Graveyard
entries: The Calamity Graveyard is a large pit of tar that contains bones from Calamity Beasts killed during the war of cinders. Squirrelfok draw their necromantic powers from here, and harvest special Corpseberries that grow in the skeletal remains.

entries.name: Forest of Iron
entries: The Forest of Iron is a forest of ancient, petrified ash trees made of iron hidden within a deep fissure. The trees still grow, their branches becoming sharp and pointed while their bark oxidizes into rust. The forest is an important source of iron for armor and weapons. The thicker the tree, the more pure the metal towards the center is, with each "tree ring" containing more and more impurities — the most ancient trees have cores of pure steel. The forest is a site of pilgrimage for lizardfolk.

entries.name: Other Locations
entries: Valley is full of a number of other villages, towns, ruins, and places of wonder. Adventure awaits!

data.name: Create Your Character
entries: When you make your D&D character, you have an array of options in the Player's Handbook to create the sort of adventurer you want. When you create an adventurer for a Bloomburrow adventure or campaign, use the options presented in this chapter.

entries.name: Choose a Class
entries: Characters of any class from the Player's Handbook can be found in Bloomburrow. {@book Chapter 5|blbcs|5} provides ideas and guidelines for how your class fits into Bloomburrow, and presents new and updated subclass options.

The subclasses presented in {@book chapter 5|blbcs|5} are designed for use with the Revised Player's Handbook (2024). If you are using a class from an older book, work with your DM to determine the appropriate character level to gain the subclass features from a subclass in this guide.

entries.name: Determine Origin
entries: Determining your character's origin involves choosing a background, a species, and two languages. When making those choices, you choose from the options presented in this chapter instead of those in the Player's Handbook.

entries.name: Choose a Background
entries: When you choose a background, choose an option presented in {@book chapter 6|blbcs|6}. If you're having trouble choosing a background, the Ability Scores and Backgrounds table shows which backgrounds benefit which ability scores. Look for your class's primary ability there.

entries.name: Other Backgrounds
entries: At the DM's discretion, if your character is a visitor to Bloomburrow from another plane of existence, you can choose a background option from another book. If that background doesn't include ability score adjustments, adjust your ability scores by increasing one score by 2 and another by 1, or increase three scores by 1. None of these increases can increase a score above 20. Also, if the background you choose doesn't provide a feat, you gain an Origin feat of your choice (see {@book chapter 7|blbcs|7}).

entries.name: Choose a Species
entries: When you choose a species for your character, choose from the species options presented in {@book chapter 4|blbcs|4}.

Even if your character is native to another plane of existence, all sapient species are transformed into one of the animalfolk species of Bloomburrow when they arrive. If your character was originally a member of a different species, they now possess only their animalfolk species' traits.

entries.name: Choose Languages
entries: Bloomburrow is not a plane with a large variety of languages, unlike most D&D worlds. Your character knows Common, the language spoken by all the animalfolk in Valley.

Some features allow a character to learn a secret language. Druidic exists on Bloomburrow as the creaking language of the Barkform and plants. Thieves' Cant is a kind of coded jargon used by Valley's bandits and criminals.

data.name: Create Your Character
entries: When you choose your character's species, choose one of the species presented in this chapter. Even if your character is from another plane of existence, all sapient creatures are magically transformed into one of Bloomburrow's animalfolk species when they arrive.

entries.name: Parts of a Species
entries: A species includes the following parts

{@b {@i Creature Type}}. A character's species determines the character's creature type. Creature types don’t have rules themselves, but some rules in the game affect creatures of certain types in different ways. The animalfolk species in this chapter are Humanoid, despite being non-humanoid animals, for the purpose of mechanical game balance.

{@b {@i Size}}. Your character's species determines the character's Size. The listed Size is a representation of the space the creature controls in combat, not necessarily their height. The actual height of a species in this chapter is listed after the species' Size.
{@b {@i Speed}}. Your character's species determines the character's Speed.
{@b {@i Special Traits}}. Each species gives a character special traits—unique characteristics based on the species' physiology or magical nature.

entries.name: Ability Scores
entries: Species in this chapter do not grant ability score adjustments. Those adjustments are instead offered by your choice of background.

entries.name: Languages
entries: Your character can speak, read, and write Common.

entries.name: Life Span
entries: The typical life span of a player character in the Magic: the Gathering multiverse is about a century, assuming the character doesn’t meet a violent end on an adventure. A species in this chapter tells you if it has an atypical life span.

entries.name: Species Desciptions
entries: 21 species are presented in this chapter in alphabetical order:

entries.name: Axolotlfolk
entries: Axolotlfolk are very rare in the lands of Valley, as their homeland is in the deep sea far beyond the Brambles. But some Axolotlfolk do travel to Valley from the marshlands via the Long River.
Axolotlfolk appear quite exotic to other animalfolk species, with blue or white skin and brightly colored frills.

entries.name: Role in Valley
entries: Unlike other cultures in Valley, Axolotlfolk primarily face aquatic Calamity Beasts as the seasons change. {@b Eluge} is perhaps one of the biggest threats to their underwater way of life.

The planeswalker Kiora is a merfolk from the plane of Zendikar. On Theros, many merfolk believed her to be the avatar of the sea goddess Thassa. While the extent of Kiora's influence on Bloomburrow is unknown, some Axoltlfolk may follow her philosophy of reverence for the larger creatures of the sea.

entries.name: Structures and Settlements
entries: Axolotlfolk spend most of their lives underwater. Their settlements are likely carved out of coral reefs found on the floor of deep bodies of water. They live alongside nonsapient animals such as fish and octopus, and may even tame some of the more dangerous aquatic life.

entries.name: Axolotlfolk Traits
entries: {@b {@i Creature Type}}: Humanoid
{@b {@i Size: Small}} (about 6-18 inches tall)
{@b {@i Speed}}: 30 feet
As an Axolotlfolk, you have these special traits.
{@b {@i Amphibious}}. You can breathe air and water.
{@b {@i Darkvision}}. You have Darkvision with a range of 120 feet.
{@b {@i Keen Senses}}. You have proficiency in the Perception skill.

{@b {@i Regeneration}}. As a Bonus Action, you can spend one Hit Point Die to regain Hit Points. Roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain Hit Points equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). This trait closes wounds and restores any missing body parts.
{@b {@i Swimming}}. You have a Swim Speed equal to your Speed.

entries.name: Badgerfolk
entries: Badgerfolk are among the less common animalfolk in Valley, and little is known of their culture. Solitary by nature, most Badgerfolk are unlikely to know much about their history either, and are unlikely to have met other Badgerfolk in their youth. Badgerfolk are also among the largest animalfolk, towering over others.

entries.name: Typical Personalities
entries: Badgerfolk have a sinister reputation of violence and aggression, but this untrue; while Badgerfolk can be formidable, they are simple and peaceable by nature, mostly keeping to themselves to forage and hunt. They are slow to trust, but when their trust can be earned, they prove most loyal companions.

entries.name: Typical Personalities
entries: Badgerfolk have a sinister reputation of violence and aggression, but this untrue; while Badgerfolk can be formidable, they are simple and peaceable by nature, mostly keeping to themselves to forage and hunt. They are slow to trust, but when their trust can be earned, they prove most loyal companions.

entries.name: Role in Valley
entries: Badgerfolk's menacing size and reputation means they can easily find work as guards, soldiers, and mercenaries. But their natural disposition leads many to become druids at home in nature, or guides to weary travelers.

entries.name: Structures and Settlements
entries: Badgerfolk aren't known to settle down as other animalfolk do, and are content to live off the land and wander for their entire lives.

entries.name: Badgerfolk Traits
entries: {@b {@i Creature Type}}: Humanoid
{@b {@i Size: Small}} (about 1–3 feet tall)
{@b {@i Speed}}: 30 feet
As a Badgerfolk, you have these special traits.

{@b {@i Bite}}. You have sharp teeth. When you use your Unarmed Strike to deal damage with them, you can deal 1d6 plus your Strength modifier Piercing damage instead of the normal damage of your Unarmed Strike.
{@b {@i Burrowing}}. You have a Burrow Speed of 5 feet.
{@b {@i Keen Senses}}. You have proficiency in the Perception skill.
{@b {@i Darkvision}}. You have Darkvision with a range of 60 feet.
{@b {@i Poison Resilience}}. You have Resistance to Poison damage. You also have Advantage on saving throws you make to avoid or end the Poisoned condition.

reaction.name: Grim Harvest (1/Turn)
entries: When the necromancer kills a creature with necrotic damage, the necromancer can take a Reaction to regain 9 ({@dice 2d8}) hit points.

reaction.name: Featherfall (1/Turn)
entries: When you fall at least 10 feet above the ground, you can take a Reaction to take 0 damage from the fall.

trait.name: Bonebound Summoning (1/day)
entries: At the start of combat, the Weaver raises two Bonebound Soldiers from the ground. These soldiers take their turns immediately after the Weaver.

trait.name: Undead Fortitude
entries: If the Weaver is reduced to 0 HP, it makes a {@dc 10} + damage taken Constitution saving throw. On a success, it drops to 1 HP instead of dying.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The Weaver makes two Bonebound Scythe attacks or casts a spell.

action.name: Bonebound Scythe
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d8 + 4}) slashing damage, plus 5 ({@damage 1d10}) necrotic damage.

action.name: Dark Tendrils {@recharge 5}
entries: Black necrotic energy lashes out in a 20-ft. cone. Creatures in the area must make a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw, taking 24 ({@damage 6d8}) necrotic damage on a failure, or half as much on a success.

A master of forbidden necromancy, this twisted Squirrelfolk channels the restless power of the Calamity Beasts' remains. With every whispered incantation, the ground churns, birthing new horrors from the tar-soaked bones of the past.

trait.name: Bonebound Camouflage
entries: If the Stalker has cover or is in dim light, it has Advantage on Stealth checks.

trait.name: Shadow Leap {@recharge 5}
entries: The Stalker can teleport up to 30 feet to an unoccupied space it can see in dim light or darkness.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The Stalker makes two Bone Dagger attacks.

action.name: Bone Dagger
entries: {@atk mw,rw} {@hit 3} to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 30/60 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 2d4 + 4}) piercing damage in melee, or 6 ({@damage 2d4 + 4}) piercing damage at range, plus 4 ({@damage 1d8}) necrotic damage.

Risen from the tar pits of the Calamity Graveyard, these skeletal soldiers move with unnatural purpose. Their bones, blackened with ancient magic, creak as they grip rusted weapons, eyes burning with eerie, necrotic light.

trait.name: Revenant’s Resilience
entries: If the Soldier takes more than 10 damage from a single hit, it gains +2 AC (up to AC 20) until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Gravebound Strike
entries: If the Soldier is within 10 feet of a tar pit or a Bonebound ally, its weapon attacks deal an additional {@damage 1d6} necrotic damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The Soldier makes two Bone Saber attacks.

action.name: Bone Saber
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d6 + 4}) slashing damage.

If the steed moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target immediately before the hit, the target takes an extra 3 ({@dice 1d6}) Bludgeoning and, if it is Large or smaller, has the Prone condition.

entries: {@atk mw} {@hit +0} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}1 piercing damage, plus 1 poison damage.

action.name: Shell Defense
entries: The snail withdraws into its shell until it emerges as a Bonus Action. While withdrawn, the snail gains a +4 bonus to AC, has the {@condition prone} and {@condition restrained} conditions, and can't take actions or Reactions.

trait.name: Water Walker
entries: he skimmer can move across water as if it were solid ground, and the skimmer knows the location of any other creature within 100 feet that is in contact with the same body of water.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit +4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}1 piercing damage.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d6 + 2}) piercing damage, and the target must make a {@dc 11} Constitution saving throw, taking 10 ({@damage 3d6}) Poison damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

trait.name: Regeneration
entries: The worm regains 2 Hit Points at the start of its turn. If it takes Bludgeoning damage, this trait doesn't function at the start of its next turn. The worm dies only if it starts its turn with 0 Hit Points and doesn't regain Hit Points.

monster.name: Baleful Strix

trait.name: Flyby
entries: The baleful strix doesn't provoke opportunity attacks when it flies out of an enemy's reach.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The strix makes two Talons attacks.

action.name: Talons
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d6 + 7}) slashing damage, and the target has the {@condition grappled} condition (escape {@dc 13}) if it is Medium or smaller.

action.name: Screech {@recharge 5}
entries: Wisdom Saving Throw: {@dc 13}, each creature within 60 feet that doesn't have the {@condition deafened} condition. Failure: 14 ({@damage 4d6}) Psychic damage, and the target has the {@condition exhaustion} condition. The strix gains Temporary Hit Points equal to the Psychic damage taken.

The Baleful Strix comes from somewhere beyond Valley. It is not an animalfolk, but neither is it a Calamity Beast. Some believe it is an interloper from another plane. It has the appearance of a great owl, but with wooden thorns protuding from its head and back, and wooden sticks with twine emerging from beneath its wings.
The strix was first sighted by squirrelfolk in the Calamity Graveyard. Whether or not the strix is the only of its kind remains to be seen.
Treasure: Arcana

alias: Render of Flesh, Devourer of Dream

monster.name: Beza

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If Beza fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Overgrowth Aura
entries: The ground in a 20-foot Emanation originating from Beza is Difficult Terrain.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Beza makes two Ram attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Ram
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 10} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d8 + 5}) bludgeoning damage. If Beza moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target immediately before the hit, the target takes an additional 4 ({@damage 1d8}) Bludgeoning damage and has the {@condition prone} condition.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: Beza casts one of the following spells, requiring no Material components, using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 17}):

reactionHeader: Beza can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Joyful Presence
entries: Trigger: Another creature Beza can see ends its turn. Response: Beza casts Charm Monster.

reaction.name: Ram Retaliation
entries: Trigger: Beza takes damage. Response: Beza moves up to half its Speed and makes one Ram attack.

Beza is an enormous Calamity Beast, an elemental elk with a petal- and leaf-covered body. With antlers made of birch trees, and flowers and fruit growing on its body, Beza is not only the herald of spring, it is the literal manifestation of it. Wherever Beza goes, spring blooms immediately in its wake.
Season of Spring

Beza brings the season of Spring. This season is characterized by a resurgence of growth and new life. It is one of the annual seasons; When Beza arrives, it drives off the Blizzard Elk of Winter, allowing plants to flourish. New life for plants also means renewed life for many hibernating insects, and these insects help pollinate many flowers; much to the chagrin of those with allergies. Many animalfolk plant their crops in Spring, hoping to reap them come the season of Harvest.
Treasure: Implements

trait.name: Chilling Aura
entries: At the end of each of the elk's turns, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the elk takes 5 ({@damage 1d10}) Cold damage. Creatures and flammable objects in the Emanation start burning. Nonmagical liquids in the Emanation that aren't being worn or carried freeze.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The elk has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The elk makes two Ram attacks.

action.name: Ram
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@damage 1d12 + 11}) bludgeoning damage plus 6 ({@damage 1d12}) Cold damage. If the elk moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target immediately before the hit, the target takes an extra 7 ({@damage 2d6}) Bludgeoning damage and, if it is Huge or smaller, has the {@condition prone} condition.

action.name: Winter's Breath {@recharge 5}
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 17}, each creature in a 60-foot Cone. Failure: 27 ({@damage 5d10}) Cold damage, and the target has the {@condition restrained} condition until the start of the elk's next turn. Failure by 5 or More: While {@condition restrained}, the target has the {@condition incapacitated} condition. Success: Half damage, and the target's Speed is reduced by 10 feet until the start of the elk's next turn.

The Blizzard Elk, also called the Galewind Moose, is a minor Calamity Beast taking the shape of a large elk or moose. Its entire body is a shade of pale green, coated with frost, and a swirling storm of snow and ice remains suspended between its antlers.
Season of Winter

The Blizzard Elk is the herald of Winter. When it arrives in Valley, the weather grows cold, snow begins to fall, and many animalfolk take to their burrows to rest and keep warm. When left unchecked, the Elk can bring winters cold enough to freeze the animalfolk, and must be driven off by those with the courage to brave the icy frost.
Treasure: Implements

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting (Cleric)
headerEntries: The priest casts one of the following spells, using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 13}):

trait.name: Echolocation
entries: The cosmos priest can't use its blindsight while {@condition deafened}.

action.name: Channel Essence
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 13}, one willing creature or one creature that has the {@condition grappled}, {@condition incapacitated}, or {@condition restrained} condition. Failure: 7 ({@damage 2d6}) Necrotic damage, and the priest gains Temporary Hit Points equal to the Necrotic damage taken.

action.name: Glaive
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit +5} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}8 ({@damage 1d10+3}) slashing damage plus ({@damage 1d6}) radiant damage.

Aside from a few cults, such the snail cult run by Wick or cults that revere the Calamity Beasts, the religion of Bloomburrow is predominantly practiced and dictated by the Batfolk. They believe their ancestors join the Cosmos in death and are assigned stars. The Batfolk pray to these ancestors for guidance, and the magic of the Cosmos can be channeled as divine magic. Many larger settlements, such as Three Tree City, have Batfolk churches which double as observatories.

trait.name: Sun Aura
entries: At the end of each of the lynx’s turns, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the lynx takes 4 ({@damage 1d8}) Radiant damage. Creatures have the {@condition blinded} condition while in the Emanation. Nonmagical plants in the Emanation wither and die.

trait.name: Illumination
entries: The lynx sheds Bright Light in a 60-foot radius and Dim Light for an additional 60 feet.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (2/Day)
entries: If the lynx fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The lynx makes two Pounce attacks.

action.name: Pounce
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 12} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d6 + 7}) radiant damage.

action.name: Hail of Cinders {@recharge 5}
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 19}, each creature within 60 feet of the lynx. Failure: 55 ({@damage 10d10}) Radiant damage, and the target has the {@condition blinded} condition until the end of its next turn. Success: Half damage only.
The Drought Cat is a Calamity Beast that appears a flaming lynx. A glowing sphere, like a radiant sun, rests on its back. As the cat moves, embers shoot off of it like laser beams of light.
Season of Drought

When the cat arrives, it brings with it a Season of Drought. It scorches the earth and ground, preventing crops from growing, and even the night is bright with radiance. Because of the nature of its shooting embers, its arrival is sometimes called the Calamity of Cinders.
Treasure: None

monster.name: Eluge

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If Eluge fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Tide Aura
entries: Water a 20-foot Emanation originating from Eluge is Difficult Terrain.

trait.name: Water Breathing
entries: The eluge can breathe only underwater.

actionHeader: Eluga makes one Slam attack and uses Bite.

action.name: Slam
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 14} to hit, reach 20 ft., one target. {@h}20 ({@damage 2d8 + 8}) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Bite
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 24}, one creature Eluge can see within 15 ft. Failure: 22 ({@damage 4d6 + 8}) Piercing damage, and the target is swallowed if it is Large or smaller. A swallowed creature has the {@condition restrained} condition, has Total Cover against attacks and other effects outside Eluge, and takes 17 ({@damage 5d6}) Acid damage at the start of each of Eluge's turns. Eluge's stomach can hold up to two creatures at a time.

If Eluge takes 30 damage or more on a single turn from a creature inside it, Eluge regurgitates all swallowed creatures, each of which falls in a space within 10 feet of Eluge has the {@condition prone} condition. If Eluge dies, all swallowed creatures are similarly regurgitated.

action.name: Tidal Wave {@recharge}
entries: Strength Saving Throw: {@dc 24}, each creature in a 200-foot Cube originating from Eluge. Failure: 45 ({@damage 71d2}) Bludgeoning damage and the target has the {@condition prone} condition. Success: Half damage only.

reactionHeader: Eluge can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Dive
entries: Trigger: Eluge uses its Legendary Resistance trait or is hit by a ranged attack.
Response: Eluge moves up to half its Swim Speed without provoking Opportunity Attacks.

reaction.name: Slam Retaliation
entries: Trigger: Eluge is hit by a melee attack.
Response: Eluge makes one Slam attack.

Also known as the Flood Gar, the Calamity Beast Eluge is enormous compared to the animalfolk. It is a 30-foot-long fish that has countless sharp, jagged teeth and long spikes protruding from its back, belly, and sides, rosy-hued magical markings running along its body, and brightly glowing blue eyes. Its giant silvery-scaled body swells the waters like a sea serpent.

Eluge is not only the herald of floods, it is the literal manifestation of them. Wherever Eluge swims, the tide swells immediately in its wake, causing rushing waters to rise and waves to crash on the shore.
Season of Deep Waters

Eluge's arrival brings the season of Deep Waters. This season is characterized by heavy rainfall and rising floods. The Long River that cuts across Valley rises considerably, and natural springs become overflowing geysers. Animalfolk that live underground are often pushed above ground, overflowing the inns of many villages. Crops are at risk of being submerged, and the Otterfolk and Frogfolk often assist with evacuation of low-lying settlements. Young ones sometimes frolic in the heavy downpours, but the adults know this can quickly become dangerous.
Treasure: Implements

trait.name: Sleep Aura
entries: Wisdom Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, any creature that starts its turn in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the ursine.
Failure: Until the start of its next turn, the target has the {@condition incapacitated} condition, its Speed is halved, and it takes a –2 penalty to its AC.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The evercoat ursine has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The ursine makes two Rend attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Rend
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 9} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}16 ({@damage 3d8 + 3}) slashing damage, and the target has the {@condition prone} condition if it is Medium or smaller.

The Evercoat Ursine is a Calamity Beast that appears as a large bear. At first glance, it might appear to have a brown fur coat, but its fur is actually made of leaves; a few of them are green in color, but most take the orange and red shades of autumn leaves.
Season of Autumn

Autumn is the season that the Evercoat Ursine brings. As it marches through the woods around Valley, trees lose their leaves and enter a deep slumber. Other creatures also feel sluggish and sleepy in the bear's presence. It emerges infrequently, spending most of its time hibernating in large caves. The Ursine is usually woken from its sleep by the passing of the {@b Sun Hawk} that brings the summer, and once he goes to sleep, the {@b Blizzard Elk} often emerges.
Treasure: Relics

trait.name: Overgrowth Aura
entries: The ground in a 20-foot Emanation originating from the greenshell is Difficult Terrain.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 12} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}28 ({@damage 3d12 + 9}) piercing damage.

action.name: Shell Defense
entries: The greenshell withdraws into its shell until it takes a Bonus Action to emerge. While withdrawn, the greenshell's AC is 22, it has the {@condition prone} and {@condition restrained} conditions, and it can't take actions or Reactions.

action.name: Seed Burst {@recharge 5}
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 16}, each creature within 15 feet of the greenshell.
Failure: 35 ({@damage 10d6}) Bludgeoning damage.
Success: Half damage.

The Fecund Greenshell is a minor Calamity Beast taking the shape of a large turtle or tortoise. It has stony skin, and its shell is covered in plants with green leaves and glowing red seeds. The greenshell is not powerful enough to bring its own season, but when it arrives, life blossoms around it. As a defense mechanism when other creatures get too close, the greenshell with fire the seeds off its shell.
Treasure: Implements

monster.name: Grotgyre

trait.name: Salt Osmosis
entries: Grotgye takes 5 ({@damage 1d10}) Necrotic damage for every 5 feet he moves in salt or for every pound of salt sprinkled on him.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Grotgyre makes two Rasp attacks.

action.name: Rasp
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}10 ({@damage 2d6 + 3}) slashing damage.

action.name: Shell Defense
entries: rotgyre withdraws into its shell until it emerges as a Bonus Action. While withdrawn, Grotgyre gains a +4 bonus to AC, has the {@condition prone} and {@condition restrained} conditions, and can't take actions or Reactions.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting (Psionics)
headerEntries: Grotgyre casts one of the following spells, requiring no components, using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 15}):

reactionHeader: Grotgyre can take the Alien Mind Reaction any number of times each round but only once per turn.

reaction.name: Alien Mind
entries: Trigger: A creature tries to read Grotgyre's thoughts or deal Psychic damage to it. Response–Intelligence Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, the triggering creature.

Failure: The target has the {@condition stunned} condition. At the end of each of its turns, the target repeats the save, ending the effect on itself on a success. After 1 minute, it succeeds automatically.

Grotgyra is a large, sapient snail with hypnotic power strong enough to bend the minds of animalfolk who get close. He has forged a psychic connection with the Ratfolk Warlock Wick, and together they command a cadre of snail-worshippers from labyrinthine tunnels below Valley. See also Wicks' statblock.
Great Old One

Grotgyre's history and origin are unknown, but his psychic power makes him an excellent Great Old One patron for a Warlock character. His servants include Snails, along with Cultists and Cult Fanatics from every animalfolk species.
Treasure: Arcana

trait.name: Stinging Aura
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, any creature that starts its turn in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the wolverine.
Failure: 10 ({@damage 4d4}) Piercing damage, and the target has the {@condition poisoned} condition until the end of its next turn.
Success: Half damage only.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The wolverine makes three Rend attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Honeyscent if available.

action.name: Rend
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}12 ({@damage 2d6 + 5}) slashing damage.

action.name: Honeyscent {@recharge 5}
entries: Wisdom Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, each creature within 30 feet.
Failure: The target has the {@condition charmed} condition until the end of its next turn.

action.name: Summon Swarm (1/Day)
entries: The wolverine summons a Swarm of Wasps, which appears in an unoccupied space within 10 feet. The swarm is an ally to the wolverine and its allies. The swarm remains for 1 hour, until the wolverine dies, or until the wolverine dismisses it as a Bonus Action.

The Hivespine Wolverine is a minor Calamity Beast. Its elemental magic is not powerful enough to bring about a new season, but it is dangerous nonetheless. Its furred body is partially made from the waxy material of beehive, and stinging bees and wasps make their nest in the wolverine's body. The insects see the beast as their home, and defend it ferociously. The wolverine is also capable of projecting sweet-smelling pheromones, which some claim it uses to draw prey out of hiding.
Treasure: Armaments

Huskbursters aren't Calamity Beasts themselves, but instead Elemental insects that are formed from the remains of fallen Calamity Beasts. They are known to burst forth from the carcasses of Calamity Beasts, hence their name, and descend as a swarm upon any who are nearby. Squirrelfolk necromancers draw power from the bones of Calamity Beasts, and so are intimately familiar with these insects; some necromancers may even be able to command them.
Treasure: Relics

trait.name: Swarm
entries: The swarm of huskbursters can occupy another creature's space and vice versa, and the swarm can move through any opening large enough for a Tiny huskburster. The swarm can't regain hit points or gain temporary hit points.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The swarm makes two Swarm of Bites attacks.

action.name: Swarm of Bites
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 6} to hit, reach 0 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 4d4 + 4}) piercing damage, or 9 ({@damage 2d4 + 4}) Piercing damage if the swarm is Bloodied.

action.name: Infest
entries: The swarm loses {@dice 1d10} Hit Points and partially infests the corpse of a Medium or smaller creature within 5 feet. In 24 hours, a Swarm of Huskbursters appears from the infested corpse. Casting Gentle Repose on the corpse ends the infestation early.

monster.name: Lumra

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the lumra fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Overgrowth Aura
entries: The ground in a 20-foot Emanation originating from Lumra is Difficult Terrain.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Lumra makes two Rend attacks and uses Bellow.

action.name: Rend
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 12} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}20 ({@damage 3d8 + 7}) slashing damage.

action.name: Bellow
entries: Wisdom Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, each creature within 120 feet that doesn't have the {@condition deafened} condition.
Failure: The target has the {@condition frightened} condition until the end of its next turn.
Success: The target is immune to Lumra's Bellow action for 24 hours.

reactionHeader: Lumra can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Erupting Forest
entries: Trigger: Lumra is hit by a ranged attack.

Response: Lumra causes a tree to burst from the ground at a point Lumra can see within 120 feet. The tree is a 5-foot-radius, 30-foot-high Cylinder, and lasts until it is destroyed (AC 5; 30 HP; Vulnerability to Fire damage; Immunity to Poison and Pyschic damage). Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 18}, one creature in the Cylinder when it appears.
Failure: 21 ({@damage 6d6}) Bludgeoning damage, and the target is lifted by the Cylinder.

reaction.name: Rend Retaliation
entries: Trigger: Lumra takes damage.
Response: Lumra makes one Rend attack.

reaction.name: Wall of Thorns
entries: Trigger: Another creature Lumra can see ends its turn.
Response: Lumra casts Wall of Thorns, requiring no Material components and using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 18}).

Lumra is a towering Calamity Beast that appears as a leaf-sprouting bear. Her eyes glow with green magic, pine cones, branches, twigs sprout from her shoulders, and her sharp teeth mark her fearsome muzzle. Long, sharp claws sprout from each paw. Wherever Lumra wanders, the landscape warps around her, causing trees to sprout and canopies to form overhead, generating a new, unrecognizable forest with a mix of deciduous and coniferous trees.
Lumra represents the feeling of getting lost in the woods, and she inspires fear among the animalfolk where she appears. Flubs is the only animalfolk known to have escaped her forests.
Season of the Maze

Lumra doesn't bring a season necessarily, but instead causes great confusion in her wake as she fundamtentally restructures woods and forests. She uproots homes and even entire villages, and causes explorers in her region to become hopelessly lost.
Treasure: Implements

monster.name: Maha

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the maha fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: Maha has Advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Night Aura
entries: Darkness extends from Maha in a 5-foot radius, and Dim Light for an additional 15 feet.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Maha makes three Talon attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Talon
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 13} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@damage 3d6 + 7}) slashing damage.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: Maha casts one of the following spells, requiring no Material components, using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 18}):

reactionHeader: Maha can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Chill Wind
entries: Trigger: Maha uses its Legendary Resistance trait or is hit by a ranged attack.
Response: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 18}, each creature in a 60-foot Cone.
Failure: 17 ({@damage 5d6}) Cold damage.
Success: Half damage.

reaction.name: Evade
entries: Trigger: Maha is hit by a melee attack.
Response: Maha flies up to half its Fly Speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

reaction.name: Rend Retaliation
entries: Trigger: Maha takes damage.
Response: Maha makes one Talon attack.

Also known as the Great Night Owl or Eclipse Owl, Maha is a fearsome Calamity Beast that takes the shape of an enormous black owl, its wingspan as broad as the branches of an oak tree. Deep blue and violet on its belly, breast, and wings transition to black-striped primary and tail feathers. Turquoise magic suffuses its beautiful, terrible form, lighting up its eyes and beak within the hollow of its face, and highlighting its deadly talons. In its wake, night follows, cutting across the sky to reveal star-flecked darkness beneath. The beast's movements are tranquil and silent, its passing marked only by the ensuing destruction and the chill wind of its wings.
Season of Long Night

When Maha flies across the sky, the Long Night follows in its wake. This season is marked by unending darkness; the sky remains dark even during the day. Many animalfolk fear this season, as it allows predators and bandits to thrive. Some folk remain indoors for nearly the entire season; though not the Batfolk, who thrive during this time and spend much time studying the stars.
Treasure: Relics

trait.name: Bloodied Fury
entries: While bloodied, the boar has Advantage on attack rolls.

trait.name: Quaking Aura
entries: The ground in a 20-foot Emanation originating from the boar is Difficult Terrain.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The boar makes two Quaketusk attacks.

action.name: Quaketusk
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 8} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}12 ({@damage 2d6 + 5}) piercing damage plus 7 ({@damage 2d6}) Force damage. If the boar moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target immediately before the hit, the target also has the {@condition prone} condition.

action.name: Quake {@recharge}
entries: The boar causes an earthquake in 60-foot Emanation originating from the boar.
Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, each creature on the ground in the Emanation.
Failure: 9 ({@damage 2d8}) Force damage, and the target has the {@condition prone} condition.
Success: Half damage only.

The Quaketusk Boar is a minor Calamity Beast. It appears as a boar with skin like molten rock, cracked with red light from beneath. Its eyes and tusks glow red with kinetic energy. Wherever the boar runs, the earth tremors like an earthquake. In the outskirts of Valley, the boar's arrival has even been known to cause landslides that can bury entire villages.
Treasure: Armaments

trait.name: Blight Aura
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, any creature that starts its turn in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the viper.
Failure: The target has the {@condition exhaustion} condition.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The viper makes one Bite attack and uses Constrict.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@damage 3d8 + 4}) piercing damage, plus 14 ({@damage 4d6}) poison damage.

action.name: Constrict
entries: Strength Saving Throw: {@dc 15}, one Large or smaller creature the viper can see within 30 feet.
Failure: 17 ({@damage 3d8 + 4}) Bludgeoning damage, and the target has the {@condition grappled} condition (escape {@dc 15}).

The Rottenmouth Viper is a minor Calamity Beast taking the shape of a large snake with long fangs and black scales. Its elemental magic is not strong enough to truly bring about a season, but it remains a dangerous enemy to the animalfolk. The giant snake causes nature to decay around it. Fighting it is said to be like fighting starvation itself.
Treasure: Implements

trait.name: Sand Aura
entries: Creatures in a 20-foot Emanation originating from the harbinger have Disadvantage on attack rolls with weapons and Wisdom (Perception) checks. A flying creature in the Emanation must land at the end of its turn or fall. The Emanation also extinguishes open flames and disperses fog.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The harbinger makes two Hooves attacks.

action.name: Hooves
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 7} to hit, reach 0 ft., one target. {@h}17 ({@damage 3d8 + 4}) bludgeoning damage. If the harbinger moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target immediately before the hit, the target takes an extra 9 ({@damage 2d8}) Bludgeoning damage and, if it is Large or smaller, has the {@condition prone} condition.

action.name: Mirage (1/Day)
entries: The harbinger casts Hallucinatory Terrain, requiring no components and using Wisdom as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 14}).

reaction.name: Retaliating Hooves
entries: Trigger: The harbinger takes damage.
Response: The harbinger makes one Hooves attack.

reaction.name: Sandy Shield
entries: Trigger: The harbinger is hit by an attack roll.
Response: The harbinger adds 3 to its AC against that attack, possibly causing the attack to miss.

The Sandcloud Harbinger is a minor Calamity Beast that appears as a large horse, with a brown coat and a white star on its forehead. Its eyes blaze with fire, and it is constantly surrounded by a sand storm. The elemental winds and sand are so strong, that if left unchecked, the land it moves through will be transformed into a desert.
Treasure: Relics

trait.name: Fiery Aura
entries: At the end of each of the hawk’s turns, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from the hawk takes 5 ({@damage 1d10}) Fire damage. Creatures and flammable objects in the Emanation start burning.

trait.name: Flyby
entries: The sun hawk doesn't provoke opportunity attacks when it flies out of an enemy's reach.

trait.name: Illumination (Azer)
entries: The sun hawk sheds bright light in a 30-foot radius and dim light for an additional 30 feet.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The hawk makes two Talons attacks.

action.name: Talons
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 10} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}14 ({@damage 2d8 + 5}) slashing damage, plus 5 ({@damage 1d10}) fire damage. If the target is a creature or a flammable object, it starts burning.

The Sun Hawk, also called the Pyreswipe Hawk, is a Calamity Beast taking the shape of a flaming bird of prey. Travelers from other planes would liken the hawk's visage to that of a phoenix; and like the phoenix, the sun hawk bears powerful elemental fire and heat.
Season of Summer

The Sun Hawk heralds the Season of Summer, bringing with it warmth and long days. Summer can be a good season of rest, but the hawk does not deal the summer heat in moderation; when it flies, fires start and spread, and the heroes of Valley must valiantly defend their homes if it flies too near.
Treasure: None

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting (Sorcerer)
headerEntries: The stormchaser casts one of the following spells, requiring no Material components, using Charisma as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 13}):

trait.name: Brave
entries: The stormchaser has advantage on saving throws against being {@condition frightened}.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The stormchaser makes two Storm Staff attacks.

action.name: Storm Staff
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 5} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}3 ({@damage 1d6}) bludgeoning damage, plus 3 ({@damage 1d6}) damage of the stormchaser's choice: Lightning or Thunder.

action.name: Stormcall {@recharge 5}
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 13}, each creature in a 15-foot Cone.

Failure: {@dice 1d8} (4) Lightning damage plus 4 ({@damage 1d8}) Thunder damage, and the target has the deafend condition until the end of its next turn. While {@condition deafened}, the target can't take Reactions.
Success: Half damage only.

From a young age, otterfolk engage in storm chasing for sport. This involves following Calamity Beasts as near as possible until the danger feels too great. Great otterfolk storm chasers have plucked the hairs from storm-bearing Beasts, and almost all otters have at least chased ﬂaming wolverine Beasts to capture embers. Otterfolk capture Calamity Beasts’ elemental magic in shells, pearls, and glass bulbs. This process is called Clamming. They have a particular fondness for lightning collected from Calamity Beasts—it seems to have a special signiﬁcance for otterball. They’re very adept with water, able to twist and bend water to distract, obfuscate, or otherwise befuddle others.
Treasure: Arcana

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Wick makes two Whorled Staff attacks. He can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Whorled Staff
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}6 ({@damage 1d8 + 2}) magical bludgeoning damage, plus 9 ({@damage 2d8}) psychic damage.

action.name: Summon Snails (1/Day)
entries: Wick summons {@dice 2d4} Snails that appear in unoccupied spaces Wick can see within 60 ft. The called creatures are allies to Wick and his allies, and remain for 1 hour, until Wick dies, or until Wick dismisses them as a Bonus Action.

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting (Warlock)
headerEntries: Wick casts one of the following spells, using Charisma as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 12}):
will: {@spell mage hand} (the hand has the invisible condition)

entries: Wick is a bent and wiry figure, draped in a purple robe and dangling adornments with mystical symbols and arcane patterns over his shoulder. His fur is a dark shade of gray, with a few silver streaks adding a touch of aged wisdom to his appearance. His piercing, violet eyes glow with an unsettling intelligence, and his whiskers twitch with an air of constant calculation. Upon his back, Wick wears a giant snail shell like an extravagant backpack, filled to the brim with knickknacks and baubles. Numerous smaller snails surround him.
Worship of the Snail

As a young pup raised in the marshland village of Conch, Wick was a prodigy amongst a community of psychics. He grew inseparable from the snail Grotgyre, their psychic bond so deeply engrained they could anticipate each other's thoughts. Then, a sickness befell the snails of Conch, and Wick along with them. He became haunted by visions of whorls and eyes, sleep eluding him. He was awake all hours of the night jotting out scripture until some years later when his paranoia ascended into enlightenment. Wick found comfort in his connection to the snails and sought to spread it. He left his village alongside a few like-minded psychics to proselytize, recruiting followers to tunnel out spiraling, labyrinthian passages into the earth where he and his converts reclused. Each new member is subjected to the same taxing initiation that Wick endured, a connection to an anointed snail forged in exchange for ultimate truth. They revere every aspect of their snails—worship them, consume their flesh, convert what's left into magic—then a new connection is forged. With it, the cycle begins anew in a never-ending spiral.
Treasure: Arcana

monster.name: Wildsear

spellcasting.name: Spellcasting
headerEntries: Wildsear casts one of the following spells, requiring no Material components, using Constitution as the spellcasting ability (spell save {@dc 19}):

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the wildsear fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

trait.name: Fiery Aura
entries: At the end of each of Wildsear’s turns, each creature in a 10-foot Emanation originating from Wildsear takes 9 ({@damage 2d8}) Fire damage. Creatures and flammable objects in the Emanation start burning.

trait.name: Illumination (Fire Elemental)
entries: The wildsear sheds bright light in a 30-foot radius and dim light for an additional 30 feet.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Wildsear makes two Bite attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Bite
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 12} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}11 ({@damage 1d8 + 7}) piercing damage, plus 7 ({@damage 2d6}) fire damage, and the the target has the {@condition prone} condition if it is Huge or smaller.

action.name: Wildfire Howl {@recharge 5}
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 19}, each creature within 60 feet.
Failure: 16 ({@damage 3d10}) Fire damage.
Success: Half damage.

reactionHeader: Wildsear can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Ignite
entries: Trigger: Another creature Wildsear can see ends its turn.
Response: One creature or flammable object Wildsear can see within 60 feet starts burning.

reaction.name: Rebuke
entries: Trigger: Wildsear is hit with an attack.
Response: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 19}, one creature Wildsear can see within 60 feet.
Failure: 11 ({@damage 2d10}) Fire damage. Success: Half damage.

Wildsear has many names: the Wildfire Wolf, the Primordial Fire, the Season of Flames; whatever his epithet, he is an infamous Calamity Beast whose giant body sets fire to everything around it. Wildsear is a giant wolf whose eyes and maw are bright with flames, and whose burning back creates a column of smoke.

Wildsear is not only the herald of wildfire, it is the literal manifestation of it. Wherever Wildsear steps, flames shoot out and catch on the surrounding landscape, causing havoc and devastation. The animalfolk say it burns through the seams of their plane and the next.
Season of Flames

The season Wildsear brings is characterized by dryness, allowing even the smallest spark to start the grandest immolation. Frogfolk work overtime to attempt to magically bring the much needed rains, and crops are at risk of drying up and dying out. Animalfolk who make their homes in trees are especially at risk of their foundations catching alight.
Treasure: None

monster.name: Ygra

trait.name: Calcifying Aura
entries: Constitution Saving Throw: {@dc 18}, any creature that starts its turn in a 10-foot Emanation originating from Ygra.
Failure: The target has the {@condition restrained} condition until the end of its next turn.
Failure by 5 or More: The {@condition restrained} target also has the {@condition petrified} condition.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the ygra fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: Ygra makes two Claw attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Devour.

action.name: Claw
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit 11} to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. {@h}22 ({@damage 3d10 + 6}) slashing damage, or Ygra deals 27 ({@damage 6d6 + 6}) necrotic damage if it had Advantage on the attack roll.

action.name: Devour
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: {@dc 18}, one creature within 5 feet.

Failure: 33 ({@damage 6d8 + 6}) Piercing damage, and the target is swallowed if it is Medium or smaller. A swallowed creature has the {@condition restrained} condition, has Total Cover against attacks and other effects outside Ygra, and takes 14 ({@damage 4d6}) Acid damage at the start of each of Ygra's turns. Ygra then gains Temporary Hit Points equal to the Acid damage taken. Ygra's stomach can hold up to two creatures at a time.

If Ygra takes 30 damage or more on a single turn from a creature inside it, Ygra regurgitates all swallowed creatures, each of which falls in a space within 10 feet of Ygra and has the {@condition prone} condition. If Ygra dies, all swallowed creatures are similarly regurgitated.

reactionHeader: Ygra can take up to three Reactions per round but only one per turn.

reaction.name: Prowl
entries: Trigger: Another creature Ygra can see ends its turn.
Response: Ygra moves up to half its Speed without provoking Opportunity Attacks.

reaction.name: Claw Retaliation
entries: Trigger: Ygra takes damage.
Response: Ygra makes one Claw attack.

Ygra is a major Calamity Beast, a giant cougar with green-glowing stripes, its muzzle marked by an unhallow light and numerous deadly teeth, and its eyes shimmer with an ominous gleam. Wherever Ygra stalks, life crumbles immediately in its wake, turning into pillars of salt.
Season of Salt

Ygra's arrival marks the season of Salt. This season is categorized by a withering of crops and the calcification of objects and structures with a build-up of salt. This is a season of dryness and drought; their is little rain, and many grow hungry. The Squirrelfolk ration their food cache stores during seasons such as this, to ensure the survival of the community.
Treasure: Implements

monster.name: Nocturne Spirit
languages: understands the languages you know and the languages it knew in life

trait.name: Leaves From
entries: The spirit can enter a hostile creature's space and stop there. It can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide.

trait.name: Plant Camouflage
entries: The spirit has Advantage on Dexterity ({@skill Stealth}) checks it makes in forest terrain.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The spirit makes a number of Cutting Leaves attacks equal to half this spell's level (rounded down).

action.name: Cutting Leaves
entries: {@atk mw} Bonus equals your spell attack modifier, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h} {@damage 2d6 + 3} + the spell's level magical slashing damage.

action.name: Flame Seed
entries: {@atk rw} Bonus equals your spell attack modifier, range 60 ft., one target. {@h} ({@damage 1d6} + PB) fire damage.

action.name: Fiery Teleportation
entries: Dexterity Saving Throw: DC equals your Spell Save DC, each creature within 5 feet.
Faliure: {@damage 1d6 +  PB} Fire damage.
Result: The spirit and each willing creature of your choice within 5 feet teleport up to 15 feet to unoccupied spaces you can see.

trait.name: Talons
entries: The birdfolk skeleton has advantage on Strength ({@skill Athletics}) checks made to climb any surface its talons could reasonably grip.

action.name: Shortsword
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit +4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}5 ({@damage 1d6 + 2}) piercing damage.

action.name: Talons
entries: {@atk mw} {@hit +4} to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. {@h}4 ({@damage 1d4 + 2}) piercing damage.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Calligrapher's Supplies, Holy Symbol (Reliquary), Navigator's Tools, Parchment (5 sheets), Robe, Scroll Case, 7 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You've devoted yourself to service in a Batfolk church or secluded grove. There, you perform rites that honor your ancestors and the Cosmos. You've served under a Cleric and studied religion. Thanks to your Cleric's instruction and your devotion, you've also learned how to channel a modicum of divine, cosmic power in service to your place of worship and the people who prayed there.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Book (history), Calligrapher's Supplies, Parchment (5 sheets), Robe, Scroll Case, Trinket, 12 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You spent your formative years in the The Knowledge Ossuary, the subterranean archive of Bloomburrow's history kept by the Ratfolk. The Long River brings flotsam of artifacts and forgotten lore to the Ossuary, and you've spent many a restless night studying trinkets and scrolls. Your mind was sharpened in the archive, and you're ready to put your knowledge to use.

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Artisan's Tools}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Artisan's Tools (same as above), Book (craftsmanship), 2 Pouches, Traveler's Clothes, 7 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You began mopping floors and scrubbing counters in an artisan's workshop for a few acorns per day as soon as you were strong enough to carry a bucket. When you were old enough to apprentice, you learned to create basic crafts of your own, how to barter, and you've developed a keen eye for detail and value.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Book (medicine), Healer's Kit, Herbalism Kit, Lamp, Oil (3 flasks), Quarterstaff, Traveler's Clothes, 11 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You've always had a level head and a knack for helping in dire situations. Perhaps you were raised in a large, busy family, or perhaps a crisis occurred when you were young that you jumped to solve. Once older, you tutored under a Cleric or folk healer, learning how to identify healing herbs and how to apply medicine and care to all the different species of Bloomburrow.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Book (food), Cook's Utensils, Fine Clothes, Oil (3 flasks), Tinderbox, Iron Pot, 6 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You practically grew up in a kitchen, helping elders by preparing meals from a very young age. You've become familiar with many ingredients, and have developed an innate intuition on how to combine them into delicious dishes. Perhaps you've found work in a restaurant in a village or roadside inn.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Crowbar, 2 Daggers, Gaming Set (choose one kind), 2 Pouches, Thieves' Tools, Traveler's Clothes, 15 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You eked out a living cutting purses or burgling shops. Perhaps you grew up in the dark alleys of a large settlement like Three-Tree City, or perhaps you turned to banditry on the roads between towns. You may have run with a mercenary gang, like Cruelclaw's infamous Direshade Company

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Musical Instrument}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) 2 Costumes, Mirror, Musical Instrument (same as above), Perfume, Traveler's Clothes, 3 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You spent much of your youth following Batfolk bards and Otterfolk acrobats, performing odd jobs in exchange for lessons. You've developed your own routine or performance, and you thrive on applause and long for the stage.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Carpenter's Tools, Healer's Kit, Iron Pot, Shovel, Sickle, Traveler's Clothes, 30 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You grew up close to the land, perhaps a Rabbitfolk garden or Squirrelfolk food reserve. Years tending insects and cultivating the earth rewarded you with patience and good health. You have a keen appreciate for nature's bounty and a healthy respect for nature's wrath.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Bedroll, Bucket, Dagger, Fishing Tackle, Navigator's Tools, Net, Trident, Traveler's Clothing, String, 12 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You have spent your life aboard fishing boats on the Long River or combing the shallows for the bounty of the ocean. Perhaps you were taught by a family of Frogfolk or Otterfolk, working to help feed your village. Regardless of how you began, you soon fell in love with the water, the art of fishing, and the promise of the eternal horizon.

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Gaming Set}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Arcane Focus (choose one kind), 5 Candles, Gaming Set (same as above), Mirror, Robe, 32 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: When you were but a child, you experienced a vision of the future that came to pass. Sensing your potential, you were brought under the wing of a Frogfolk auger or Batfolk stargazer, and learned the patterns behind the chaos of the movement of the stars and Calamity Beasts. You've also been taught how to divine the future with the use of arcane tools. Your adventure begins when you receive another vision...

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Gaming Set}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Bell, Crossbow Bolt Case, Gaming Set (same as above), Hooded Lantern, Light Crossbow, 20 Bolts, Manacles, Oil (3 flasks), Signal Whistle, Spear, Traveler's Clothes, 10 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You've spent countless hours in your post at the guard tower in a large settlement, perhaps Three-Tree City or Fountainport. You were trained to keep one eye looking outside the wall, watching for signs of Calamity Beasts, and your other eye looking inside the wall, searching for bandits and troublemakers.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Bedroll, Blanket, Cartographer's Tools, Map (choose one area), Quiver, Shortbow, 20 Arrows, Tent, Traveler's Clothes; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You came of age outdoors, far from settled lands. Your home was anywhere you chose to spread your bedroll. There are wonders in Valley's wilderness—strange insects, Calamity Beasts, overgrown ruins—and you learned to fend for yourself as you explored them. From time to time, you've found work as a courier, wilderness guide, or insect hunter.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Brewer's Supplies, Fine Clothes, 3 Glass Bottles, Iron Pot, Oil (3 flasks), Tinderbox, 6 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You spent much of your youth scrubbing floors and counters at a local inn for spare coin, and you soon became a popular regular fixture at the establishment. You were taken under the wing of the establishment's owner, and were taught how to run the business and sweet talk your patrons. You've even learned a few recipes for brewing spirits.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Block and Tackle, Cart, Navigator's Tools, 2 Pouches, Traveler's Clothes, 6 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You were apprenticed to a trader, caravan master, or shopkeeper, learning the fundamentals of commerce. You traveled broadly, and you earned a living by buying and selling the raw materials artisans need to practice their crafts, or finished works from such crafters. You might have transported goods from one place to another (by ship, wagon, or caravan) or bought them from traveling traders and sold them in your own shop.

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Gaming Set}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Fine Clothes, Gaming Set (same as above), Mirror, Perfume, 24 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: There is no single authority in Valley, with individual settlements having mayors or councils. You were raised as the heir of one such nobleman, surrounded by wealth, power, and privilege. Your family ensured that you received a first-class education in history and diplomacy, and it was always expected that you would one day fulfill a position of leadership.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Block and Tackle, Dagger, Navigator's Tools, Pole, Rope, Traveler's Clothes, 18 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You lived on the Long River that cuts across Valley, wind at your back and decks swaying beneath your feet. You were raised alongside Otterfolk sailors, or perhaps pirates, and perched on barstools in more ports of call than you can remember. Along the river you've faced mighty storms, enormous river creatures, and even pirate crews.

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Bedroll, 2 Daggers, Hooded Lantern, Oil (3 flasks), 2 Pouches, Shovel, Thieves' Tools, Traveler's Clothes, 9 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: You grew up on the fringes of society, perhaps on the streets of a settlement like Three-Tree City, or perhaps on the outskirts of villages. You slept where you could and did odd jobs for food. You've learned to take what's been discarded or left unused, never letting anything go to waste.

items.name: Tool Proficiencies
items.entry: {@i Choose one kind of Gaming Set}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: {@i Choose A or B:} (A) Gaming Set (same as above), Healer's Kit, Quiver, Shortbow, 20 Arrows, Spear, Traveler's Clothes, 14 GP; or (B) 50 GP

entries.name: Overview:
entries: Every settlement in Valley needs to be ready for Calamity Beasts, and many companies of mercenaries and knights form to stand vigilant. You began your martial training with one such group as soon as you reach adulthood and carry precious few memories of life before you took up arms. Battle is in your blood, and you put your training to use on the battlefield, protecting Valley by waging war.

feat.name: Chef
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Cook's Utensils
entries: You gain proficiency with Cook's Utensils if you don't already have it.

entries.name: Replensihing Meal
entries: As part of a Short Rest, you can cook special food if you have ingredients and Cook's Utensils on hand. You can prepare enough of this food for a number of creatures equal to 4 plus your Proficiency Bonus. At the end of the Short Rest, any creature who eats the food and spends one or more Hit Dice to regain Hit Points regains an extra 1d8 Hit Points.

entries.name: Bolstering Treats
entries: With 1 hour of work or when you finish a Long Rest, you can cook a number of treats equal to your Proficiency Bonus. These special treats last 8 hours after being made. A creature can use a Bonus Action to eat one of those treats to gain Temporary Hit Points equal to your character level plus your Wisdom modifier.

feat.name: Crafter
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Tool Proficiency
entries: You gain proficiency with three different Artisan's Tools of your choice from the Fast Crafting table.

entries.name: Discount
entries: Whenever you buy a nonmagical item, you receive a 20 percent discount on it.

entries.name: Fast Crafting
entries: When you finish a Long Rest you can craft one piece of gear from the Fast Crafting table, provided you have the Artisan's Tools associated with that item and have proficiency with those tools. The item lasts until you finish another Long Rest, at which point the item falls apart.

entries.name: Fast Crafting
rows: Ball Bearings, Caltrops, Grappling Hook, Iron Pot

feat.name: Musician
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Instrument Training
entries: You gain proficiency with three different Musical Instruments of your choice.

entries.name: Song of Courage
entries: When you finish a Short or Long Rest, you can play a song using a Musical Instrument with which you have proficiency. A number of allies equal to your Proficiency Bonus are given Heroic Inspiration.

feat.name: Ritual Augur
entries: You learn the {@spell Augury} spell. You always have it prepared, and you can cast it using any spell slots you have of appropriate level. Wisdom is the spell's spellcasting ability.

feat.name: Sonar Striker
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Ability Score Increase
entries: Increase your Constitution score by 1, to a maximum of 30.

entries.name: Thunder Resistance
entries: You have Resistance to Thunder damage.

entries.name: Searing Shriek
entries: While you have Blindsight from your Echolocation trait and take the Attack action on your turn, you can replace one attack with a sonar screech in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in that area that doesn't have the deafened condition must make on a Constitution saving throw (DC 8 plus your Constitution modifier and Proficiency Bonus), taking 1d10 Thunder damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

feat.name: Stormchaser
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Ability Score Increase
entries: Increase your Dexterity or Intelligence score by 1, to a maximum of 30.

entries.name: Pearl of Wisdom
entries: When you finish a Long Rest, you can transform a Pearl, Bottle, Trinket or other Tiny item you touch into a {@i Ring of Spell Storing}. You can only have one {@i Ring of Spell Storing} created with this benefit at a time; creating a new one causes the first to lose all its magic.

entries.name: Clamming
entries: When a spell attack misses you or you succeed on a saving throw against a spell that targets only you, if you are holding or wearing a {@i Ring of Spell Storing}, you can take a Reaction to store that spell in the ring if it can hold the spell.

feat.name: Windcrested
entries: You gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Ability Score Increase
entries: Increase your Dexterity score by 1, to a maximum of 30.

entries.name: Flyby
entries: You don't provoke Opportunity Attacks when you use your Fly Speed to move out of an enemy's reach.

entries.name: Speedy
entries: Your Speed increases by 10 feet.

spell.name: Ascend
entries: You touch an object or portion of solid earthen ground no larger than 3 feet in diameter that weighs no more than 200 pounds. The object can't be worn or carried, and the ground can't be in a space occupied by a creature other than you. The spell causes the target to float above the ground for the duration, and the target can magically support up to 200 pounds. If more weight is placed on it, the spell ends, and the target and everything on it falls to the ground.
